prompt: In nmap, what does the `-sV` option do?

options.text: Probe open ports to determine the service and version running
Perform a host-discovery (ping) sweep without scanning ports
Attempt to identify the remote operating system

explanation: `-sV` is service/version detection: nmap interrogates open ports to fingerprint the service and its version. `-sn` is ping sweep, `-p-` scans all ports, and `-O` is OS detection.
source_reference: nmap reference (man nmap): Service and Version Detection

prompt: In nmap, what does the `-sn` option do?

options.text: Perform host discovery (ping sweep) without a port scan
Detect the service version on open ports
Run the default NSE script set

explanation: `-sn` is a 'no port scan' host-discovery sweep — it tells you which hosts are up without scanning their ports. `-sV` does version detection, `-sC` runs default scripts, `-sU` scans UDP.

options.text: Scan all 65535 TCP ports rather than just the default top ~1000
Scan only the single most common port

explanation: `-p-` expands the scan to every TCP port (1-65535). By default nmap scans only the top ~1000 ports, so services on unusual ports are missed without `-p-`.

prompt: In nmap, what does the `-O` option do?

options.text: Attempt to fingerprint the remote operating system
Detect the version of each network service
Scan only open ports found earlier

explanation: `-O` enables OS detection via TCP/IP stack fingerprinting. Note `-sV` (lowercase, service version) is different from `-O` (operating system).
